summary: Joe Reis argues that the AI hype cycle resembles televangelism, with tech leaders promising imminent AGI while soliciting massive investments. He notes that even prominent AI evangelists are now hedging their predictions, while enterprise AI pilots show a 95% failure rate on ROI metrics, yet companies paradoxically continue laying off workers 'because AI.'
key_points: AI bubble is unprecedented in scale, dwarfing the dot-com era
Tech leaders like Altman, Suleyman, and Schmidt are beginning to hedge their AGI predictions
MIT report shows 95% of enterprise GenAI pilots have no measurable ROI
Contradictory narratives: AI simultaneously 'doesn't work' and 'is taking jobs'
AI faces the same adoption barriers as previous modernization efforts
money_quotes: You've got preachers on stage telling us the Lord AGI is coming down from the sky any day now. But salvation is expensive, so please send in your checks.
When the evangelists start hedging, you know the revival tent is getting drafty.
You can't have both 'AI doesn't work' and 'AI is coming for your job' at the same time. The crossed signals are dizzying.
AI is running into the same walls as every modernization effort before it - messy organizations, unclear success metrics, and slow adoption curves.
The televangelists aren't done passing the plate. The Church of AGI is still open for donations.
key_insight: The AI industry's contradictory narratives—simultaneously claiming AI doesn't deliver ROI while also replacing workers—reveal that hype is outpacing reality, and even AI's biggest promoters are starting to hedge.
